text: Predictive modeling of chemistry at the atomistic scale has become an increasingly important tool in many areas of science and engineering from medicine to energy production. As ab initio methods remain computationally expensive to use for large systems, efficient machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have become an alternative to perform simulations of atomistic systems with high accuracy and computational speed. It is therefore highly desirable for modeling reactive chemistry in a “model free” fashion, that is, a general reactive MLIP for a large class of chemistry reactions that can be used as a black-box potential without the need for parameter reoptimization for each unique system being studied. Here, we obtain a general reactive MLIP—dubbed ANI-1xnr—by training on a dataset that was generated through an automated workflow for sampling a variety of condensed-phase reactions. We evaluate the performance of ANI-1xnr across five systems including solid carbon nucleation, ring formation on graphene from acetylene, optimization of biofuel additives, and combustion of methane as well as the spontaneous formation of glycine from primitive Earth abundant small molecules. The ANI-1xnr potential is found to yield results that are in good agreement with experimental observations and simulations based on traditional models when such data are available. The broad applicability of ANI-1xnr to reactive systems in the condensed phase for C, H, N, and O atoms makes it a highly general purpose tool for predictive modeling of reactive chemistry and facilitates the high-throughput study of reactions in silico.
